title: Standard Uniplex PCR SOP for Antibiotic Resistance Genes
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"9t4kk","text":"This protocol describes the procedures on how to set-up polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and run gel electrophoresis for selected antibiotic resistance genes and determinants: sulfonamide (sul1), erythromycin (erm(B)), extended spectrum β-lactamase (blaCTX-M-1), integrase (intI1) (aka 4-Gene Uniplex); and tetracycline tet(A), tet(O), and tet(X). ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":188,"length":4},{"style":"italic","offset":209,"length":3},{"style":"italic","offset":249,"length":3},{"style":"italic","offset":273,"length":5},{"style":"italic","offset":319,"length":3},{"style":"italic","offset":327,"length":3},{"style":"italic","offset":339,"length":3},{"style":"sub","offset":252,"length":7}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"13qtf","text":"Scope","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":5}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4gkjt","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5ho4d","text":"Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) is a technique that enables researchers to rapidly and efficiently amplify (copy) a specific region of DNA (Deoxyribonucleic Acid) or RNA (Ribonucleic Acid) into millions of copies. A simple PCR reaction consists of target DNA, a set of synthetic oligonucleotide primers that flank the target DNA sequence, a thermostable DNA polymerase (usually Taq polymerase), and nucleotides. Thermal cycling exposes these contents to repeated cycles of heating and cooling that promotes DNA replication. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":378,"length":3}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2a42l","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3r3eq","text":"There are three steps involved in the process: ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1tlj8","text":"Denaturation ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fi34k","text":"Annealing","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"73c37","text":"Elongation ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8pknj","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ceqd6","text":"A suite of antibiotic resistance determinants were selected as a general screening tool for agricultural and environmental substrates using a series of standard uniplex PCR reactions. The three tetracycline resistance genes chosen here tet(A), tet(O), and tet(X) represent the three main tetracycline resistance mechanisms of action: efflux (A), ribosomal protection (O), and enzymatic (X). The additional four genes are grouped together as the 4-gene uniplex (4GU) set of targets.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":236,"length":3},{"style":"italic","offset":244,"length":3},{"style":"italic","offset":256,"length":3}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"eqkr9","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9ej4f","text":"Responsibility","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":14}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"di5av","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"eh4q1","text":"This SOP applies to all staff members and students. These individuals must be knowledgeable about the requirements set forth within this document. The lab manager or designee shall ensure that all staff and students know the proper techniques. steps: If frozen, thaw all reagents and samples and keep on an ice block or On ice for stability. If kept in the refrigerator, keep there until ready for use, and keep on an ice block or On ice for stability when in use.
Label tubes for the samples and controls.
Prepare the PCR Prep area for setting up a PCR reaction to eliminate contamination – UV PCR tubes, PCR water, and microcentrifuge tube for at least 00:15:00 .
Combine the Master Mix components for the number of reactions (allow for 10% extra Master Mix) using the recipes from Table 1 with the correct primer sets ( Table 2 ) for 25µL reactions. %s %s %s %s
Before adding to the Master Mix – mix the primers by aspirating and expelling with the pipette tip.
Vortex the completed Master Mix for at least 00:00:20 . Centrifuge briefly. %s %s
Check the pipette’s volume as you go along, making sure it is exactly at the correct volume.
Carefully pipette 5 µL of PCR Water to the non-template control ( NTC ) tube.
Add 20 µL of Master Mix to each labeled 8-tube PCR strip.
Next, gently vortex to mix then pipette 5 µL of sample (5µL of working stock; or 1µL straight DNA + 4µL PCR water) into its same labeled tube.
Use a new pipette tip for each sample.
To make a working stock dilution, see SOP: IDT Primer and gBlock Hydration and Aliquots.
Add 1 µL of positive control ( Table 3 ) (mix first by aspirating and expelling with the pipette tip) to its same labeled tube.
The correct concentration of gBlock (10 6 /5µL – (or whatever concentration is working for your lab)) should already be pre-made and stored at -20 °C .
Centrifuge the strip tubes so that the entire volume is at the bottom.
Make sure the centrifuge is balanced with both sides containing tubes.
Load the tubes into the Thermocycler, keeping them in order.
Find the programmed gene conditions and double check that they match the conditions listed in Table 2 .
Run PCR.
When cycle is complete:
Gel electrophoresis (See section 'Procedure - Gel Electrophoresis' go to step #28 ) OR
Store the tubes in a labeled rack at -20 °C until ready to run a gel.
Turn off the Thermocycler.

If frozen, thaw all reagents and samples and keep on an ice block or On ice for stability. If kept in the refrigerator, keep there until ready for use, and keep on an ice block or On ice for stability when in use.
Label tubes for the samples and controls.
Prepare the PCR Prep area for setting up a PCR reaction to eliminate contamination – UV PCR tubes, PCR water, and microcentrifuge tube for rpm, °C, 15 minutes .
Combine the Master Mix components for the number of reactions (allow for 10% extra Master Mix) using the recipe from Table 4 with the correct primer sets in Table 5 for 20 µL reactions. %s %s %s %s
Before adding to the Master Mix - mix the primers by aspirating and expelling with the pipette tip.
Vortex the completed Master Mix for at least 00:00:20 .
Check the pipette’s volume as you go along, making sure it is exactly at the correct volume.
Carefully pipette 5 µL of PCR Water to the non-template control (NTC) tube.
Add 15 µL of Master Mix to each labeled PCR tube.
Next, vortex to mix then pipette 5 µL of sample (5µL of working stock; or 1µL straight DNA + 4µL PCR water) into its same labeled tube.
Use a new pipette tip for each sample.
To make a working stock dilution, see SOP: IDT Primer and gBlock Hydration and Aliquots.
Add 1 µL of positive control (mix first by aspirating and expelling with the pipette tip) to its same labeled tube. See Appendix A (in Guidelines) for sequences.
Centrifuge the strip tubes so that the entire volume is at the bottom.
Make sure the centrifuge is balanced with both sides containing tubes.
Load the tubes into the Thermocycler, keeping them in order.
Find the programmed gene conditions and double check that they match the conditions listed in Table 5 (in Step 18) .
Run PCR.
When cycle is complete:
Gel electrophoresis (See section 'Procedure - Gel Electrophoresis' go to step #28 ) OR
Store tubes in a labeled rack at -20 °C until ready to run a gel.
Turn off the Thermocycler.
Using a spoon, measure out 2.6 g of powdered agarose into a weigh boat.
Measure 260 mL of 1X SB buffer into a graduated cylinder.
Add powdered agarose and 1X SB into a 1 L bottle and swirl to mix.
Microwave SB and agarose mixture for 00:01:00 with bottle lid LOOSE.
Remove bottle from microwave ( using hot-pad gloves ), tighten the lid and gently swirl.
LOOSEN the lid and put in microwave for 00:01:00 .
Check mixture visually, if flecks are present repeat go to step #32 , loosen lid and microwave again for 00:00:30 .
Add 26 µL of SYBR safe to the molten gel.
Swirl gently with a loose lid to distribute the SYBR.
Place bottle in 55 °C water bath for 00:10:00 to temper.
While the mixture is tempering, set up gel cast by putting two plastic stoppers into the ends of the cast, with the orange rubber side of the stopper facing out.
Put two (or three) 36-well combs into the first and fourth notches of the gel cast (gel cast is right side up if first notch is close to the top of the cast).
After the gel has tempered, slowly pour it ( using hot pad gloves ) into the gel cast.
If there are any noticeable bubbles, take a lighter and hold it over the bubble, which should make it pop.
Cover gel cast loosely with foil and allow it to set (up to an hour) before use.
Electrophoresis Conditions
10 µL of PCR amplicon will go into a well, flanked by 5 µL of ladder. Fill out an electrophoresis worksheet to have a map of the wells.
Run at 120 Volts for 02:00:00 in 1x SB buffer.
Make sure SB gel goes into a gel boat with SB buffer .
Remove processed gel and analyze using a gel imaging system.
Samples with a positive band will be confirmed on qPCR ( See SOP: 5-Gene Uniplex Antibiotic Resistance qPCR SOP ).
Directions to make 10 liters of 1x SB Buffer Stock : Prepare a clean, 10 L carboy.
In a graduated cylinder, measure out 500 mL of 20x SB, and pour into the carboy.
In a graduated cylinder, measure out 9500 mL of dH 2 O, and pour into the carboy.
Put the cap on tight, and swirl gently to mix.
Autoclave the carboy.
Place autoclave tape on the cap, and write SB on the tape.
Put carboy in a large autoclave bin.
Loosen the cap.
Autoclave at 121 °C .
Label, and cool to Room temperature before use.
creator: Justine C. Condon
